content.text: The persona section of the system prompt tells the model who it is for this run. It is not decoration — it sets the model's domain vocabulary, its expectations about output depth, and what it treats as within scope. A vague persona ("helpful AI assistant") leaves the model without a frame of reference; a specific persona ("backend engineer debugging a FastAPI payment service") grounds every response in relevant domain knowledge and constrains the kinds of answers the model will offer.

attrs.alt: Persona and role — three components and their effect on model behaviour

content.text: The Three Components of a Persona
An effective persona has three components, each of which shapes a different aspect of model behaviour:
Component
What it sets
Example
Domain
Vocabulary, background knowledge, what counts as a fact vs a guess
"Python FastAPI payment service"
Function
What the agent does in this run; distinguishes it from other agents
"fix the bug described in the task"
Scope
How deep or broad the work is; implicit expectations about output
"minimal fix; do not refactor"
All three components can fit in one or two sentences. Longer is not better — a persona that runs to a paragraph dilutes the constraints and environment sections that follow it.
Structured Role Definition
A dataclass that separates domain, function, and scope makes it easy to vary each component independently when configuring different agents:
domain is what the agent knows
— the technology, service, or problem area. It sets the vocabulary the model uses and the background knowledge it draws on.
function is what it does here
— distinct from domain. In a multi-agent system, two agents can share the same domain but have different functions (coder vs reviewer).
scope is how far it goes
— "minimal fix only", "root cause, not symptom", "full refactor allowed". Scope sets implicit expectations about output length and depth.
Why Persona Specificity Matters

# A persona is three things in one sentence:
# Domain — what the agent knows about
# Function — what it does in this run
# Scope — how deep or broad its work is

# VAGUE — model has no frame of reference
BAD_PERSONA = "You are a helpful AI assistant that helps with code."

# SPECIFIC — domain + function + scope in 30 words
GOOD_PERSONA = (
    "You are a backend engineer debugging a production 500 error "
    "in a Python FastAPI payment service. "
    "Your job is to identify the root cause and propose a minimal fix."
)
# Domain:   Python FastAPI, payment service
# Function: debugging → root cause analysis
# Scope:    minimal fix (not refactor, not new features)
A vague persona produces generic responses
— without domain context, the model defaults to broad advice. It cannot know whether "keep it simple" means 10 lines or 100, or whether a third-party library is acceptable.
A specific persona sets implicit constraints

— "minimal fix" signals the model should not refactor. "payment service" signals it should handle currency and rounding carefully. These carry information that would otherwise require explicit constraints.
Specificity compounds with goal clarity
— a specific persona plus verifiable success criteria (from the goals section) together eliminate most common agent failure modes: scope creep, vague output, and premature completion.
Personas in Multi-Agent Systems

When multiple agents collaborate, each needs a persona that makes its function exclusive. An orchestrator that also codes, a reviewer that also writes tests — these role overlaps cause confusion in multi-turn interactions. Each agent's function should be non-overlapping and stated as a prohibition in the others' prompts:
Shared domain, distinct function
— all agents in a pipeline can share the same domain description. The function is what distinguishes them. This prevents the orchestrator from doing the coder's work.
Explicit prohibitions
"Do not write code yourself"
in the orchestrator prompt is as important as the delegation instruction. Without it, the model may attempt to do the work directly.
Output format is part of the function
— the reviewer's
"VERDICT: APPROVE or REQUEST_CHANGES"
is not an output format constraint, it is part of its function definition. It tells the model what a completed review looks like.
Domain-Specific Persona Patterns

Different domains require different framings. An incident responder needs speed and containment; a security auditor needs adversarial thinking; a documentation writer needs audience awareness. The persona sets these defaults before the task is even read:

# Domain-specific persona patterns

# 1. Incident responder — emphasises speed and minimal blast radius
INCIDENT_PERSONA = (
    "You are a site reliability engineer triaging a production incident. "
    "Identify the immediate cause of the alert and propose a mitigation. "
    "Prioritise containment over root cause; do not make changes that could worsen the outage."
)

# 2. Security auditor — emphasises adversarial thinking
SECURITY_PERSONA = (
    "You are a security engineer auditing a Python web service for vulnerabilities. "
    "Review the code for OWASP Top 10 issues, injection risks, and authentication flaws. "
    "Report each issue as: SEVERITY | CATEGORY | DESCRIPTION | FILE:LINE."
)

# 3. Data analyst — emphasises accuracy over speed
ANALYST_PERSONA = (
    "You are a data analyst working with a PostgreSQL database for a retail business. "
    "Write SQL queries to answer the question in the task. "
    "Prefer accuracy over speed. Include a comment explaining each clause."
)

# 4. Documentation writer — emphasises audience and format
DOCS_PERSONA = (
    "You are a technical writer documenting a Python library for developers who are new to it. "
    "Write clear, example-driven docstrings and README sections. "
    "Assume the reader knows Python but not this library."
)
Incident responder
— "containment over root cause" is a scope constraint that only makes sense in this context. It prevents the model from going deep when going fast matters.
Security auditor
— a structured output format (SEVERITY | CATEGORY | DESCRIPTION | FILE:LINE) is part of the persona because it reflects how security findings are communicated professionally.
Documentation writer
— stating the audience ("developers new to this library") affects vocabulary, the level of assumed knowledge, and whether to include motivation or jump straight to examples.
Embedding the Role in the Agent Loop
Common Pitfalls

# PITFALL 1 — too generic (model behaves like a general chatbot)
BAD = "You are a helpful coding assistant."
GOOD = "You are a Python engineer fixing a bug in a Django REST API. Keep the change minimal."

# PITFALL 2 — domain and function mixed up
# Domain = what you know; Function = what you do here
BAD = "You are an expert at fixing bugs."          # function disguised as domain
GOOD = "You are a Python engineer. Fix the bug."   # domain + function separated

# PITFALL 3 — contradictory scope instructions
BAD_SCOPE = [
    "Fix the bug thoroughly.",
    "Keep changes minimal.",
    "Refactor the module while you're in there.",
]
# 'thorough fix' and 'minimal change' conflict; 'refactor' contradicts 'minimal'.
# Pick one scope and state it once.
GOOD_SCOPE = "Fix only the bug. Do not refactor or clean up unrelated code."

# PITFALL 4 — role inflation (assigning too broad a remit)
# An orchestrator that also does coding, reviewing, and testing is not an orchestrator.
# Each agent's function should be exclusive and non-overlapping.
BAD_ORCHESTRATOR = (
    "You are an orchestrator. Delegate tasks AND implement code AND write tests."
)
GOOD_ORCHESTRATOR = (
    "You are an orchestrator. Delegate coding to 'coder', testing to 'tester'. "
    "Do not write code or tests yourself."
)
Too generic
— "helpful assistant" is the default the model already has. A persona that does not add information wastes tokens.
Domain and function conflated
— "expert at fixing bugs" is a function masquerading as a domain. Separate them: domain = what you know, function = what you do.
Contradictory scope
— "thorough" and "minimal" cannot both be true. State one scope and mean it.
Role inflation
— giving an orchestrator coding, testing, and reviewing responsibilities defeats the purpose of multi-agent decomposition. Each function should be exclusive.
Testing Persona Logic
Summary

A persona has three components: domain (what the agent knows), function (what it does in this run), and scope (how deep or broad). All three can fit in one or two sentences. Specific personas outperform vague ones because they set vocabulary, implicit constraints, and output depth before the model reads the task. In multi-agent systems, make each agent's function exclusive and state prohibitions explicitly — an orchestrator that does not write code needs to be told not to write code. Scope statements ("minimal fix only", "root cause not symptom") set implicit output expectations that would otherwise require explicit constraints. Keep the persona section short so constraints and environment details read clearly after it.
